prompt_md: What is the primary benefit of C++20 Concepts over pre-C++20 SFINAE template constraints?

options.a: Concepts make templates run faster at runtime.
options.b: Concepts produce clear, constraint-violation error messages at the call site rather than deep inside template instantiation output.
options.c: Concepts allow templates to work with runtime polymorphism.
options.d: Concepts automatically generate default implementations for missing operations.

explanation_md: SFINAE errors are famously hard to read because the compiler reports a failure buried inside template instantiation chains. Concepts produce a single, readable error at the call site stating which constraint was violated. They also serve as self-documenting requirements visible in the function signature.

Defaulting `operator<=>` synthesizes all six comparison operators using member-wise comparison. `a.n` (5) > `b.n` (3), so `a > b` is `true` (prints `1`). `5 != 3`, so `a == b` is `false` (prints `0`).

prompt_md: What does a C++20 range view (such as `std::views::filter`) return when applied to a vector?

options.a: A new std::vector containing only the matching elements.
options.b: A lazy wrapper that produces matching elements on demand without allocating storage.
options.c: An iterator pointing to the first matching element.
options.d: A std::span over the matching subset of the original vector.

explanation_md: Range views are non-owning, lazy adaptors. They wrap the original range and generate elements one at a time when iterated — no new container is allocated. This is why you cannot call `push_back` on a view and why chaining multiple views is still efficient: each element is touched at most once during the final iteration.

prompt_md: When is it appropriate to use `std::span<const int>` as a function parameter instead of `const std::vector<int>&`?

options.a: When you want the function to own its own copy of the data.
options.b: When the function needs to work with any contiguous sequence — vectors, arrays, and subranges — without requiring the caller to create a vector.
options.c: When the function needs to append elements to the sequence.
options.d: Only when the data is stored in a C-style array.

explanation_md: `std::span<const int>` accepts any contiguous range: raw arrays, `std::vector`, `std::array`, or a subrange thereof. This is more flexible than `const std::vector<int>&` which forces the caller to have a vector. The function sees a uniform (pointer, length) pair under the hood, so it works identically regardless of the source container.
